Malleswari4, Arghya Banerjee5 and Rahul Sadhukhan6 1ICAR- National Academy of Agricultural Research Management (NAARM), Hyderabad-500030, Telangana 2ICAR-Central Research Institute for Dryland Agriculture (CRIDA), Hyderabad-500059, Telangana 3ICAR- Research Complex for North Eastern Hill Region (RCNEHR), Meghalaya-793103 4AICRP on Agro meteorology, Agricultural Research Station, ANGRAU, Ananthapuram-515001, Andhra Pradesh 5Department of Plant Pathology, Bidhan Chandra Krishi Viswavidyalaya, Mohanpur-741252, West Bengal 6Central Agricultural University, Imphal, Selesih-796015, Mizoram Abstract Agriculture is significantly affected with weather and so the appropriate planning with pertinent and dependable evidences are necessary for better crop production. crop weather calendar (CWC) covers concise facts and figures of crops in single sheet of paper and explain different farm activity strategies according to the predominant weather situations in fields. However, CWC is static in nature and they fail to produce the desired results and become obsolete with fast changing crop varieties. Based on the risks perceived in farm sector and to protect the farmers against weather related risks, Ministry of Agriculture, in 1985, announced the crop insurance scheme (CIS). On the basis of experiences gained and views of stakeholders, CIS was improved from time to time. In India, majority of farmers are small holders, the threat and vagueness accompanying with farming is tremendous. Under these conditions, agricultural insurance is one of the most reasonable means of risk management. Still, in India, CIS is at tender stage and not extensive due to several influences including limited farm holdings size, lack of consciousness, insurance cost, basis risk, insufficiency of consistent data, and scalability of technology. Based on the above circumstances, making use of dynamic crop weather calendars and executable CIS can increase the decision making and enhance self-assurance among the farming community of this country.
